qa_findings.title: Browse button gives no feedback when the OS folder picker is unavailable
qa_findings.impact: On a headless / no-Tk box, choose_directory_dialog returns {ok:false, path:"", error:"folder picker unavailable: ...; paste the path into the box instead"} — a deliberately recoverable message. But browseInto only acts on `if(j.ok && j.path)`; when ok is false it does nothing: no toast, no error, no fallback hint. The button looks completely dead and the helpful 'paste the path' guidance is swallowed. (The endpoint itself is correctly wired — choose_directory_dialog is reachable via the app star-import chain, so no NameError — and dialog-cancel returning path:'' correctly leaves the field unchanged.)
qa_findings.fix: In browseInto, surface the failure: `if(j.ok===false){toast(j.error||'folder picker unavailable — paste the path');return;} if(j.path) el(id).value=j.path;`.
qa_findings.verify_note: Confirmed. choose_directory_dialog returns {ok:false, path:'', error:'folder picker unavailable: ...; paste the path into the box instead'} on no-Tk boxes (earcrate/core/util.py:172,182) with HTTP 200 (server.py:266). Since the response is 200, post()/api() do NOT throw (api throws only on !r.ok, index.html:155), so no toast fires there. browseInto only acts on `if(j.ok&&j.path)` (index.html:578); on ok:false it does nothing and the recoverable 'paste the path' error is swallowed. Endpoint wiring itself is correct (choose_directory_dialog imported/reachable, cancel returns path:'' leaving the field unchanged). Medium missing-error-state.

qa_findings.title: Analysis depth is shown as a tunable setting but has no control to change it
qa_findings.impact: The Performance card renders 'Analysis depth - seconds/track' with a filled progress bar and '180s' label, reading cfg.analysis_seconds. But there is no input/slider/button to change it anywhere in the UI (grep confirms only the query box uses oninput; no range input, no setDepth). saveWorkspace/config_workspace never send analysis_seconds, and configure_workspace only forwards it when data.analysis_seconds is truthy — which the UI never provides. So analysis depth is permanently pinned at the default 180s from the UI; the widget looks adjustable but is inert.
qa_findings.fix: Add an editable control (range input) that posts analysis_seconds via config_workspace/config, or relabel the bar as read-only/informational so it doesn't imply it can be tuned.
qa_findings.verify_note: Confirmed. renderSetup computes depth=cfg.analysis_seconds||180 (index.html:553) and renders a filled bar + '180s' label (index.html:564-566). Grep for analysis_seconds in index.html returns only line 553 (the read); there is no range input, no setDepth, no button that posts analysis_seconds. saveWorkspace sends only {music_folder, workspace_folder} (index.html:580). configure_workspace forwards analysis_seconds only when data.analysis_seconds is truthy (app.py:769), which the UI never provides, so depth is pinned at DEFAULT_ANALYSIS_SECONDS. The widget looks adjustable but is inert.

qa_findings.title: Toggling workers before a workspace is saved throws a raw KeyError toast
qa_findings.impact: If the user clicks 'FULL SEND'/'GENTLE' before saving a workspace, toggleWorkers computes cur=(d.configured||{}).workers (undefined->0), next=4, and posts /api/config with `{...(d.configured||{}), workers:4}` = just {workers:4}. configure() immediately does data['master_root'] which raises KeyError, returning a 500; post() surfaces it as the toast "X 'master_root'". Confusing raw error instead of a 'save a workspace first' guard.
qa_findings.fix: In toggleWorkers, bail if not configured (toast 'save a workspace first'), or have configure() raise a clear ValueError('master_root is required — configure a workspace first') instead of a bare KeyError.
qa_findings.verify_note: Confirmed. When unconfigured, /api/defaults returns configured:null, so toggleWorkers computes cur=(d.configured||{}).workers||0 -> 0, next=4, and posts /api/config with {...(d.configured||{}), workers:4} == {workers:4} (index.html:581). configure() immediately dereferences data['master_root'] (app.py:515) -> KeyError. The server's except returns 500 with {'error': str(exc)} == "'master_root'" (server.py:340,348), api() throws Error(j.error) (index.html:155), post() toasts '✕ '+e.message (index.html:157) -> "✕ 'master_root'". Confusing raw error; no 'save a workspace first' guard exists.

qa_findings.title: 'Migrate legacy' is a dead-end: preview is discarded and there is no way to apply
qa_findings.where: index.html:571 (button) -> index.html:546 libAction -> server.py:322 /api/migrate/plan -> app.py:892 plan_workspace_migration
qa_findings.impact: The button posts /api/migrate/plan, which returns a rich dry-run preview (actions[], summary, human text, signature). But libAction collapses the whole response to a single toast: since the plan has no `planned` field, the user just sees 'plan: DRY-RUN - ok'. The action list, human summary and signature are thrown away, and there is no UI to review the plan or to call /api/migrate/apply (which additionally requires passing that signature). The migration feature is unreachable from the UI. Also, before any workspace is configured the same call raises ValueError('workspace_folder ... is required') -> 500 -> toast.
qa_findings.fix: Route migrate/plan to a dedicated renderer that shows homes/actions/human and an Apply button that re-posts /api/migrate/apply with the returned signature; guard the unconfigured case.
qa_findings.verify_note: Confirmed. Button posts /api/migrate/plan via libAction (index.html:571). plan_workspace_migration returns {ok, dry_run:true, actions, summary, human, signature, ...} (app.py:911-915) but has NO 'planned' field. libAction collapses it: `j.planned!=null? ... : (j.ok!==false?'ok':...)` -> since planned is undefined, and dry_run true, the toast is exactly 'plan: DRY-RUN — ok' (index.html:546). actions/human/signature are discarded. Grep confirms index.html never references migrate/apply, so /api/migrate/apply (server.py:323, which additionally requires the signature) is unreachable from the UI. Unconfigured case: _migration_actions raises ValueError('workspace_folder (or music_folder to derive the sibling) is required') (app.py:882-883) -> 500 -> toast. Feature effectively unreachable.

qa_findings.title: Returning to Workbench visually erases the current plan while Save/Render still act on it
qa_findings.where: index.html:370-385 renderWorkbench (sets wbPlan to 'no plan yet', calls renderPre([]) and renderPipe(null)); window.__plan persists in memory
qa_findings.impact: renderWorkbench rebuilds the panel to 'no plan yet' and never re-renders the in-memory window.__plan when you navigate away and back. But window.__plan and window.__plans persist, so Save plan still silently saves the old (now-invisible) plan and Render still runs. The user sees 'no plan yet' yet Save succeeds — confusing and can save/render a stale plan they thought was gone.
qa_findings.fix: In renderWorkbench, if window.__plan exists call renderPlanRails(window.__plan) (and restore the meta/pre-gate state) instead of hard-resetting to 'no plan yet'.
qa_findings.verify_note: go(mode) at index.html:184 calls the mode's render fn on every navigation. renderWorkbench (370-385) rewrites scr.innerHTML with wbPlan='no plan yet' and calls renderPipe(null)/renderPre([]) without ever reading the persisted window.__plan. window.__plan is a window global set by proposePlan/loadPlan, so it survives navigation: savePlan (387) still saves it and renderMix (429) still fires, while the user sees 'no plan yet'. Confirmed missing-state bug. (Note: Render itself ignores window.__plan per F1, so the 'stale save' is the real hazard; the 'stale render' framing is moot but harmless.)

qa_findings.title: No busy-guard on Book a set / Render; concurrent click 500s and bookSet strands the user on Play
qa_findings.where: bookSet index.html:280-284, renderMix index.html:429; run_background raises "EarCrate is already busy" app.py:5219-5220
qa_findings.impact: If a run is already in progress, clicking Book a set or Render hits run_background, which raises RuntimeError("EarCrate is already busy"). The POST handler turns that into a 500 (app.py:340-348) and post() shows a transient toast "✕ EarCrate is already busy". For bookSet the throw also skips go('activity') (both are inside the same try, index.html:283), so the user is left on Play with only a 6-second toast and no indication a run is ongoing. The buttons are never disabled while busy despite the app exposing status.busy.
qa_findings.fix: Disable/relabel Book a set and Render while status.busy is true, and/or have bookSet navigate to Activity even on the busy error so the user sees the in-progress run.
qa_findings.verify_note: run_background raises RuntimeError("EarCrate is already busy") when status.busy (app.py:5218-5220). do_POST's except returns _json(500,{error,trace}) (server.py:340-348). api() throws on !r.ok (index.html:155), post() toasts "✕ "+message then rethrows (index.html:157). In bookSet the throw occurs at the await inside the same try as go('activity') (index.html:283), so go('activity') is skipped and the user is left on Play with only a 6s toast. Neither button consults status.busy to disable itself. Confirmed low.

qa_findings.title: Synchronous /api/one_click can return a raw 500 with a stack trace
qa_findings.where: server.py:303-304 → one_click_mix; outer except re-raises app.py:3428-3439
qa_findings.impact: one_click_taste_mix's outermost handler re-raises after bookkeeping, so the synchronous /api/one_click route returns HTTP 500 with {error, trace} on any hard failure (e.g. doctor failure raising RuntimeError at app.py:3318). This endpoint is not wired to any button on this surface (bookSet and renderMix both use /api/one_click_bg, which swallows the exception into status), so it is latent, but the naked trace leaks if anything calls the sync route.
qa_findings.fix: Have the sync route return the structured refusal dict instead of re-raising, mirroring the _bg path, or wrap it so hard errors become {ok:false,error} rather than a 500+trace.
qa_findings.verify_note: server.py:303-304 wires /api/one_click straight to one_click_mix (no run_background). one_click_taste_mix's outer handler re-raises after bookkeeping (app.py:3428-3439, bare `raise` at 3439), and hard failures like the doctor RuntimeError (app.py:3318) hit it; do_POST turns that into 500 {error,trace} (server.py:348) leaking the traceback. Confirmed the route is unused by this surface: grep of index.html shows only /api/one_click_bg is called (lines 283,429), never /api/one_click -- so it is latent, not live. Confirmed low.

qa_findings.title: playDemo swallows all failure/empty outcomes, leaving a stuck "Synthesizing…" toast
qa_findings.impact: playDemo fires a "Synthesizing a warm-up demo set…" toast, then does everything inside a try/catch(e){} with no else/failure branch. If /api/demo/seed 500s (this surface calls numpy/soundfile at app.py:3726-3740, which can raise), or if endlessQueue returns 0 items (e.g. seed was skipped because real renders exist AND those renders were all filtered out by the current_engine/quality_gate check), the function silently exits with the optimistic "Synthesizing…" toast still on screen and nothing playing. The user has no signal that anything failed.
qa_findings.fix: Add an else branch when items.length is 0 and surface caught errors, e.g. toast('Could not seed demo — '+ (e.message||'no playable renders')).
qa_findings.verify_note: Partially confirmed with an important correction to the reasoning. CORRECT: playDemo (index.html:599) fires the optimistic 'Synthesizing…' toast, then wraps everything in try/catch(e){} with an `if(items.length){...}` and NO else — so when endlessQueue() returns 0 items (seed skipped because real renders exist per app.py:3711-3716, AND those renders are all filtered out by endlessQueue's `r.current_engine && r.quality_gate_passed!==false` check at index.html:595), the function exits silently with the stale toast and nothing playing. That missing empty-state is real. REFUTED PART: the tester claims a /api/demo/seed 500 also gives 'no signal.' It does give a signal — post() (index.html:157) calls api(), which throws on !r.ok (index.html:155), and post()'s catch fires `toast('✕ '+e.message)` before rethrowing into playDemo's empty catch. So server errors ARE surfaced by post()'s own toast; only the empty-queue edge is truly swallowed. Because the sole unhandled path is a narrow edge case and errors are already toasted, I'd assign low rather than medium.

qa_findings.title: Demo seed count is hardcoded to 8 with no seed-count input
qa_findings.impact: There is no seed-count input anywhere; the DEMO control always requests count:8. The backend accepts and clamps count (app.py:3747, max 1..len(progs)=10), so the plumbing supports a variable count, but the UI never exposes it. Minor — the fixed 8 is a reasonable default — but worth noting the count parameter is effectively vestigial from the UI's perspective.
qa_findings.fix: Optional: expose a small count selector, or leave as-is and document that demo count is fixed.
qa_findings.verify_note: Verified. DEMO control (index.html:95) calls playDemo(); playDemo posts {count:8} (index.html:599) with no UI input anywhere. Backend does honor a variable count: seed_demo_renders clamps `n = max(1, min(len(progs), int(count)))` at app.py:3747, and progs (app.py:3721-3723) has exactly 10 entries, so the effective range is 1..10 — the count param is real plumbing but vestigial from the UI's perspective. Correctly rated low; 8 is a reasonable fixed default.

qa_findings.title: Forced ear-crate rebuild would silently drop non-locked approve/reject judgments (persisted row is never re-applied)
qa_findings.where: app.py:2603-2633 _upsert_atom (only locked_ids preserve status); app.py:5119-5123 set_atom_judgment writes atom_judgments AND ear_atoms.status
qa_findings.impact: build_ear_crate with force=True re-measures in place and, via ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE SET status=excluded.status, overwrites ear_atoms.status with a freshly classify_atom_status()-computed value. Only atoms in locked_ids (locked=1) keep their human status/role; a plain Reject or Approve (locked=0) is reclassified and the persisted atom_judgments.status is never re-applied to ear_atoms. list_ear_atoms/approved_atom_pool/rank read ear_atoms.status, not atom_judgments, so a forced rebuild resurrects rejected atoms. Note: NOT reachable from this surface's UI — the Build button posts force:false (index.html:495), and the incremental path skips loops already in ear_atoms (app.py:2542), so under the UI's own flow reject/approve DO survive rebuild. Flagged because the code comment claims judgments survive 'every rebuild', which is only true for locked ones.
qa_findings.fix: On rebuild, after upsert, re-apply persisted atom_judgments.status/ear_role for the profile (not just locked=1), or have the read path COALESCE ear_atoms.status with atom_judgments.status.
qa_findings.verify_note: CONFIRMED as a code fact, and the finding correctly scopes it. On force=True, build_ear_crate re-measures in place and _upsert_atom (app.py:2603-2627) writes status via ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE SET status=excluded.status. Only atoms in locked_ids (locked=1) preserve their prior ear_role/status (app.py:2608-2610); a plain non-locked Reject/Approve gets overwritten by the freshly classify_atom_status-computed value, and atom_judgments.status is never re-applied to ear_atoms. Read paths (approved_atom_pool, app.py:2771) read ear_atoms.status, so a forced rebuild resurrects a rejected atom. As the tester notes, this is NOT reachable from this surface: the Build button posts force:false (index.html:495 -> server.py:282), and the code comment at app.py:2540-2541/2891-2895 claiming judgments 'survive every rebuild' is only true for locked ones. Low severity is fair — a real doc/behavior mismatch but not triggerable from the crate UI's own flow.

qa_findings.title: Header skin badge is hardcoded '//LATTICE' and never follows the active skin
qa_findings.impact: The command-strip badge next to the EARCRATE wordmark is a literal '//LATTICE' string. applySkin() only rewrites #app's CSS vars and the #skinStrip buttons (index.html:169) — it never touches this badge. Selecting SIGNAL, TERMINAL, or PAPER recolors the UI and highlights the correct SKIN button, but the header still reads '//LATTICE', contradicting the active theme.
qa_findings.fix: Give the badge an id and set its text to SKINS[S.skin].name inside applySkin(), or remove the hardcoded skin name.
qa_findings.verify_note: Confirmed. index.html:37 is a literal '//LATTICE' span with no id. applySkin() (index.html:166-173) only rewrites #app's inline style vars and rebuilds #skinStrip buttons; it never touches this badge. Selecting SIGNAL/TERMINAL/PAPER recolors the UI and highlights the right SKIN button but the wordmark badge still reads '//LATTICE'. Cosmetic — low is right.

qa_findings.title: Top chrome (NOW / crate-fit / seed) only refreshes when the Play screen renders
qa_findings.where: index.html:198 updateChrome (called only from renderPlay index.html:213)
qa_findings.impact: updateChrome() populates fitPct/fitBar/nowTxt/seedTxt and is invoked only inside renderPlay. pollStatus (2s) updates onlineDot/onlineTxt/engineTxt but not these fields. So after navigating away from Play, the NOW resident and crate-fit percentage in the global command strip freeze at whatever the last Play render produced, even if the active resident or readiness changes (e.g. after a compile completes). Cosmetic but the strip is presented as a live readout.
qa_findings.fix: Call updateChrome() from pollStatus (or on resident changes) so the global strip stays current regardless of the active screen.
qa_findings.verify_note: Confirmed. grep shows updateChrome is defined at index.html:198 and called only once, from renderPlay at index.html:213. pollStatus (2s, index.html:603-610) updates onlineDot/onlineTxt/engineTxt but never fitPct/fitBar/nowTxt/seedTxt. After navigating off Play these strip fields freeze at the last Play render even if readiness/active resident changes. Cosmetic stale-readout — low.

qa_findings.title: A stale/unknown stored skin leaves S.skin invalid with no active highlight
qa_findings.impact: S.skin is taken verbatim from localStorage without validating against SKINS. If a persisted value no longer exists (e.g. a skin renamed across versions), applySkin falls back to SKINS.lattice for the CSS vars but the #skinStrip highlight loop (on = k===S.skin, index.html:170) matches nothing, so no button appears selected while the app renders in lattice — a confusing mismatch, and S.skin stays the invalid string until the user clicks a swatch.
qa_findings.fix: Normalize on read: if !SKINS[stored] fall back to 'lattice' and rewrite localStorage, so S.skin always names a real skin.
qa_findings.verify_note: Confirmed. index.html:146 sets S.skin = localStorage.getItem('ec_skin')||'lattice' with no validation against SKINS. applySkin uses `SKINS[S.skin]||SKINS.lattice` for CSS vars (index.html:167) but the #skinStrip highlight uses `on = k===S.skin` (index.html:170), so an unknown persisted value renders lattice colors while NO swatch shows selected, and S.skin stays the invalid string until a click. Real but only reachable if a persisted skin name is later removed/renamed across versions — robustness edge, low.

qa_findings.title: api() can throw an empty error message on an empty or bodyless error response
qa_findings.impact: On an error response whose body is empty or non-JSON, JSON.parse('') throws and j becomes {raw:''}; then `j.error||txt` evaluates to '' (index.html:155), so `throw new Error('')`. post() then toasts just '✕ ' with no message (index.html:157), giving the user a blank error. Edge case (most backend errors carry a JSON body), but produces a meaningless toast when it does occur.
qa_findings.fix: Fall back to a status-based message when both j.error and txt are empty, e.g. `throw new Error(j.error || txt || (r.status+' '+r.statusText))`.
qa_findings.verify_note: Confirmed at code level. index.html:154 does `let txt=await r.text(); let j; try{j=JSON.parse(txt)}catch(e){j={raw:txt}}` then line 155 `throw new Error(j.error||txt)`. For an empty body: txt='', JSON.parse('') throws, j={raw:''}, j.error is undefined, so j.error||txt === '' → `throw new Error('')`. post() (line 157) then toasts '✕ ' with no message. Reachability caveat: every backend error via _json() carries a JSON body, and the http.server default send_error path returns a non-empty HTML body (which would yield a non-blank raw error), so the truly-blank case is an edge condition — matching the tester's own 'edge case' framing and low severity. The code logic producing the blank toast is unambiguously present.

qa_findings.title: apply=true Ingest/Organize results are toasted as 'N planned', reading like a dry-run even though files were written
qa_findings.impact: libAction's toast is (j.dry_run?'DRY-RUN — ':'')+(j.planned!=null?j.planned+' planned':...). Both ingest_sources and organize_and_retag return `planned:len(ops)` on the apply=true path too, so a real, executed organize/ingest toasts e.g. 'organize: 12 planned'. The only distinguishing signal is the ABSENCE of the 'DRY-RUN —' prefix; the word 'planned' still implies nothing was written.
qa_findings.fix: On the apply path report the executed count (e.g. result.done length) with wording like 'N copied/organized', reserving 'planned' for dry-runs.
qa_findings.verify_note: libAction toast (index.html:546) = (j.dry_run?'DRY-RUN — ':'')+(j.planned!=null?j.planned+' planned':...). On the apply path ingest_sources returns planned:len(ops) with dry_run:not apply=False (ingest.py:190,193) and organize_and_retag returns planned:len(ops) with dry_run:False (ingest.py:315,319). So a real executed organize toasts e.g. 'organize: 12 planned' — the only distinguishing signal from a dry-run is the absence of the 'DRY-RUN —' prefix, while the word 'planned' still implies nothing was written. Confirmed. Low confirmed.

qa_findings.title: 'Approve' and 'Lock' chips are the identical action (both approve+lock)
qa_findings.impact: Both 'Approve' and 'Lock' chips call loopSetStatus(id,'approved'). loopSetStatus posts /api/loop_status with only {loop_id,status}; set_loop_status defaults locked=True, so status='approved' always sets locked=1. The two chips therefore do exactly the same thing and both fire the same toast ('approved + locked (survives quota)'). 'Lock' presents itself as a distinct capability but is a duplicate button — there is no UI path to approve-without-lock, which is the only thing 'Lock' vs 'Approve' could meaningfully distinguish.
qa_findings.fix: Remove the redundant 'Lock' chip, or make the two paths differ (e.g. plain approve vs explicit lock) by threading a locked flag through loopSetStatus/the endpoint.
qa_findings.verify_note: index.html:531 — both chip('Approve','approved',...) and chip('Lock','approved',...) call loopSetStatus(id,'approved'). loopSetStatus (535) posts {loop_id,status} only. server.py:276 calls set_loop_status(loop_id,status) with no locked arg, so app.py:2188 locked defaults True and status=='approved' sets locked=1 (2213-2214). Both chips therefore do byte-identical work and fire the same 'approved + locked' toast. No UI path exists to approve-without-lock, so 'Lock' is a pure duplicate. Confirmed; low is appropriate (redundant, not broken).

qa_findings.title: Quota-approve toast/count overcounts by re-counting already-locked approvals
qa_findings.impact: chosen is seeded with locked_rows (loops already status='approved', locked=1), and ids = list(chosen.keys())[:max_loops] therefore includes those locked loops, which executemany re-sets to 'approved'. The returned approved=len(ids) counts these pre-existing approvals as if newly promoted, and the UI toast reports 'Quota approved N loops -> hot pool' using j.approved. With locked approvals present, N overstates how many loops actually changed to approved.
qa_findings.fix: Return the count of loops that actually transitioned to approved (exclude already-approved locked ids), or label the number as 'hot pool size' rather than 'approved'.
qa_findings.verify_note: app.py:2388 seeds chosen with locked_rows, whose query (2381) selects status='approved' AND locked=1 — loops already approved. ids=list(chosen.keys())[:max_loops] (2422) thus includes them, and executemany re-sets them to 'approved' (2424, a no-op transition). The return approved=len(ids) (2435) counts these pre-existing locked approvals, and index.html:537 shows them via j.approved in 'Quota approved N loops'. When prior human-locked approvals exist, N overstates loops that actually changed to approved (it is really hot-pool size). Confirmed; low.

qa_findings.title: Volume indicator shows 85% while actual volume is 100%
qa_findings.impact: The volume bar is hardcoded to 85% width at load, but the <audio> element's default volume is 1.0 (100%) and setVol() is only invoked on click. Until the user touches the volume control, the displayed level (85%) does not match the audible level (100%).
qa_findings.fix: On boot, set AP().volume to 0.85 to match the bar (or set the bar width from AP().volume).
qa_findings.verify_note: index.html:117 hardcodes volBar width:85%. Grep for '.volume' shows setVol (593) is the ONLY writer of AP().volume and it fires only on click — no boot code initializes volume, so the <audio> keeps its default 1.0. Until the user clicks the strip, bar (85%) mismatches audible level (100%). Confirmed, low.

qa_findings.title: Malformed Range header can 500 the audio endpoint
qa_findings.impact: In _send_file, a Range like 'bytes=abc-' or 'bytes=-xyz' reaches int(a)/int(b) and raises ValueError, which propagates to the do_GET except handler and returns a 500 with a full traceback instead of a 416/200. Edge case (well-behaved browsers send valid ranges), but a malformed/hand-crafted request breaks playback with a server error.
qa_findings.fix: Wrap the range-spec parse in try/except and fall back to a full 200 response (or 416) on parse failure.
qa_findings.verify_note: server.py:114-117 does int(a)/int(b) with no guard. A Range like 'bytes=abc-' passes startswith('bytes=') (110), spec='abc-', '-' in spec, a='abc' truthy -> int('abc') raises ValueError. _send_file is called at do_GET line 230 inside the try, and the parse happens before any send_response, so the exception propagates cleanly to the except at 233 and returns _json(500, {error, trace}) with a full traceback (236) instead of 416/200. Confirmed, low (only hand-crafted/malformed ranges; browsers send valid ones).

qa_findings.title: Readiness panel never surfaces the backend's explanatory failures[] strings
qa_findings.impact: taste_readiness builds human-readable failure strings like "foreground atoms short: have 3, need 8" and returns them in `failures` (and `ready`). renderReadiness ignores both `rd.failures` and `rd.ready`; it only re-derives per-row ok from have/need and colors the bar. The per-role have/need bars do convey WHICH role is short, so it isn't a fully dead state, but the ready/not-ready verdict and the composed failure copy the backend produced are dropped. This is the exact 'failures[] not explained' concern in the report — the strings exist server-side but the UI never shows them.
qa_findings.fix: Render rd.failures (and an overall rd.ready badge) in the readiness card, e.g. a small list under the bars when !rd.ready.
qa_findings.verify_note: Verified. taste_readiness builds human-readable strings like 'foreground atoms short: have X, need Y' into `failures` and returns both `failures` and `ready` (app.py:2831-2834, 2839). renderReadiness (index.html:355-367) reads only rd.have, rd.need, and rd.endless; it re-derives per-row ok from have/need and never references rd.failures or rd.ready. The per-role have/need bars do show which role is short, so it is not fully dead, but the composed failure copy and the overall ready verdict are dropped. Matches the code exactly.

qa_findings.title: Readiness have{} double-counts multi-role atoms, so 'ready' can overstate real inventory
qa_findings.impact: The same ear atom is counted into multiple buckets: RIFF_ID counts toward both foreground and floor; TEXTURE toward both floor and spark; VOX_SHOUT toward both foreground and spark. A crate composed mostly of, say, TEXTURE atoms can satisfy both the floor and spark needs simultaneously from one physical set of atoms, so readiness can report ready/no failures while the composer cannot actually staff floor and spark concurrently. This may be intentional (an atom can play multiple roles), but combined with the readiness panel being the user's 'am I ready' signal it can green-light a pool that the arranger can't realize.
qa_findings.fix: If atoms truly can't serve two rails at once in a given section, count each atom toward one bucket (or track distinct-atom budgets), or document that buckets are non-exclusive so the readiness verdict isn't read as guaranteed simultaneous coverage.
qa_findings.verify_note: The double-counting is provable from app.py:2825-2828: RIFF_ID is summed into both `foreground` and `floor`; TEXTURE into both `floor` and `spark`; VOX_SHOUT into both `foreground` and `spark`. The buckets are demonstrably non-exclusive — one physical atom is counted toward multiple role needs, and `ready` (line 2839) is derived from these buckets vs need. The mechanical defect (non-exclusive counting) is confirmed. The downstream impact ('the arranger cannot staff floor and spark concurrently') depends on whether an atom may serve two rails in a section, which is a runtime/design question not settleable from this method alone — but that nuance does not change the confirmed miscount. Severity low is appropriate.

qa_findings.title: Build-pair-graph '0 edges' success gives no guidance
qa_findings.impact: build_compatibility_graph returns {ok:True, edges:0} when every candidate pair scores below min_edge_score (0.54). buildGraph then toasts 'Graph: 0 edges' — a success-styled message with no explanation that nothing passed the compatibility threshold, and no next step. The empty-pool case is handled (ok:False -> j.error), but the 'built, but nothing qualified' case reads as success.
qa_findings.fix: Special-case edges===0 in buildGraph with an explanatory toast (e.g. 'no pairs cleared min_edge_score — crate atoms are keys/tempos apart or same-source').
qa_findings.verify_note: Verified. build_compatibility_graph returns {ok:True, ..., edges:made} where `made` starts at 0 and only increments when a pair scores >= min_edge_score (default 0.54, app.py:2905). A non-empty pool where nothing clears the threshold returns {ok:True, edges:0}. buildGraph (index.html:428) does `toast(j.ok?('Graph: '+j.edges+' edges'):(j.error||'graph failed'))`, so edges:0 renders as the success-path toast 'Graph: 0 edges' with no explanation or next step. The empty-pool case is separately handled via ok:False -> error (app.py:2879-2880). Confirmed as a UX gap.

qa_findings.title: Status poll and most Sessions/Activity actions swallow all errors silently
qa_findings.impact: pollStatus's catch{} is empty, so a persistent 500/403 on /api/status leaves the header dot/text frozen at their last value with no indication anything is wrong — it does NOT wedge the loop (good: setInterval keeps firing and there is no backoff needed), but it also never surfaces the failure. Similarly renderSessions (line 434) catches and leaves items=[] silently, and judgeRender/execManifest swallow errors. Not a crash, but a stuck status indicator is misleading.
qa_findings.fix: In pollStatus catch, degrade the indicator (e.g. set onlineTxt to 'CONNECTION LOST' / grey dot) so a dead server is visible.
qa_findings.verify_note: pollStatus has an empty `catch(e){}` (index.html:609); on a persistent 500/403 from /api/status the header dot and onlineTxt stay frozen at their last value with no failure indication. setInterval keeps firing so the loop is not wedged (tester's nuance is accurate). renderSessions (434), judgeRender (450), and execManifest (452) likewise `catch(e){}` with no user-visible degrade. Provable and correct, though low severity — a stuck-but-not-crashed indicator.

qa_findings.title: Doctor drops stem-capability note and per-check detail
qa_findings.impact: doctor() returns {checks:[{name,ok,detail}], config, stem_capability:{...,note}} but runDoctor renders only 'name' with a check/cross. The per-check `detail` (actual paths, 'not writable: ...' reasons, sqlite error text) and the honest stem_capability note ('stem separation OFF - needs torch+demucs...') are never shown, so a failing check gives no diagnostic context. (The core shape does match — j.checks + c.ok + c.name — so the panel itself works.)
qa_findings.fix: Append esc(c.detail) after the name on failing checks, and render stem_capability.note as an informational line.
qa_findings.verify_note: Confirmed. doctor() returns {checks:[{name,ok,detail}], config, stem_capability:{...,note}} with rich detail on every check (paths, 'not writable: <exc>', sqlite error text) and an honest note ('stem separation OFF — needs torch+demucs...') (app.py:1356-1389). runDoctor renders only `esc(c.name||c.detail||'')` with a check/cross (index.html:582); since name is always truthy, detail is never shown, and stem_capability.note is never rendered at all. A failing check gives no diagnostic context. Core shape (j.checks + c.ok + c.name) matches, so the panel renders; this is lost-info/polish, low severity.

qa_findings.title: Manifest op-count never displays (field name mismatch ops vs operations)
qa_findings.where: index.html:451 loadManifests reads m.ops; app.py:3696 list_manifests returns key 'operations'
qa_findings.impact: The manifest row builds its meta line with (m.ops!=null?m.ops+' ops · ':''). The backend returns the count under 'operations', never 'ops', so m.ops is always undefined and the '<N> ops ·' prefix never renders. Users never see how many operations a manifest contains before previewing/executing it.
qa_findings.fix: Read m.operations (or add an 'ops' alias in list_manifests) in loadManifests.
qa_findings.verify_note: loadManifests at index.html:451 builds the meta line with (m.ops!=null?m.ops+' ops · ':''). list_manifests at app.py:3696 emits the count under key 'operations' (len of data.get('operations')), never 'ops'. m.ops is therefore always undefined and the '<N> ops ·' prefix never renders. Confirmed shape mismatch. Downgrading to low: it only suppresses an informational prefix on a meta line; core preview/execute still work.

qa_findings.title: Manifest human summary is dropped; every manifest labeled by raw filename
qa_findings.where: index.html:451 loadManifests reads m.label||m.type||m.name; app.py:3696 list_manifests returns 'summary' and 'manifest_id'
qa_findings.impact: The row title falls back through m.label/m.type/m.name — none of which list_manifests emits — to p.split('/').pop() (the filename). The informative 'summary' the backend actually provides is never shown, so all manifests are identified only by their opaque JSON filenames.
qa_findings.fix: Include m.summary (and/or m.manifest_id) in the label fallback chain, or rename the backend field to one the frontend reads.
qa_findings.verify_note: loadManifests (index.html:451) labels each row with m.label||m.type||m.name||p.split(/[\/]/).pop(). list_manifests (app.py:3696) returns only path, manifest_id, summary, created_at, operations — it emits none of label/type/name, so the chain always falls through to the filename. The 'summary' the backend provides is never displayed. Confirmed.

qa_findings.title: Re-saving a loaded plan under a different resident forks a duplicate instead of updating
qa_findings.where: app.py:5197-5202 save_plan; index.html:387 savePlan passes taste_profile:S.resident
qa_findings.impact: save_plan overwrites plan['tastespec'] with load_tastespec(taste_profile) then recomputes arrangement_sha. If you loadPlan a plan saved under profile A while S.resident is now B, savePlan rewrites the tastespec to B, changing the plan_hash, so INSERT OR REPLACE keyed on plan_hash creates a NEW row rather than updating the existing plan. Silent duplication / hash drift on round-trip re-save across residents.
qa_findings.fix: Preserve the plan's embedded tastespec on save (only inject when absent), or derive taste_profile from the plan itself rather than the current UI resident.
qa_findings.verify_note: save_plan (app.py:5197-5202) overwrites plan['tastespec'] with load_tastespec(taste_profile)'s {id,version,hash} then computes ph=arrangement_sha(plan). arrangement_sha (core/util.py:234-235) is sha256 over json_dumps of the ENTIRE dict, so the embedded tastespec is part of the hash. savePlan (index.html:387) always passes taste_profile:S.resident. Loading a plan saved under profile A while S.resident=B rewrites tastespec to B, producing a new plan_hash; the INSERT OR REPLACE COALESCE((SELECT id FROM saved_plans WHERE plan_hash=?)) subquery finds no row at the new hash, so a fresh id is inserted, leaving the original A row intact. Confirmed silent duplication/hash-drift on cross-resident re-save.

qa_findings.title: Backend list/profile endpoints for this surface are dead relative to the crate UI
qa_findings.where: server.py:182 /api/ear_atoms (GET); server.py:195 /api/taste/profile (GET); app.py:2726 list_ear_atoms, app.py:5099 taste_profile_receipt
qa_findings.impact: The crate surface populates its list from POST /api/rank and its readiness from POST /api/taste/readiness. GET /api/ear_atoms and GET /api/taste/profile are never called anywhere in index.html for this surface (only POST /api/ear_atoms/judgment is used). Not a defect, but the list/counts/profile-receipt shapes those endpoints return are unused here, so field-shape drift in them would go unnoticed.
qa_findings.fix: None required; note for coverage. If the counts/receipt data is desired in the UI, wire it in; otherwise it is harmless.
qa_findings.verify_note: CONFIRMED. GET /api/ear_atoms (server.py:182 -> list_ear_atoms) and GET /api/taste/profile (server.py:195 -> taste_profile_receipt) are never called from index.html; the only /api/ear_atoms reference in the UI is the POST /api/ear_atoms/judgment (index.html:351). The crate list uses POST /api/rank and readiness uses POST /api/taste/readiness. Not a defect — just unused shapes on this surface whose field drift would go unnoticed. Polish is fair.

qa_findings.title: 'Organize' (apply) action is styled identically to its 'Preview' dry-run, unlike Ingest's primary-styled 'Ingest now'
qa_findings.impact: Ingest marks its real action with the primary flag (['Ingest now',...,1]) but Organize marks both Preview and the real Organize with 0, so the destructive/writing action has no visual emphasis and looks the same as the safe preview. Inconsistent with the Ingest card and easy to click by accident.
qa_findings.fix: Give the real 'Organize' action the primary (a[2]=1) styling, matching 'Ingest now'.
qa_findings.verify_note: index.html:484 defines Organize actions as [['Preview',...,0],['Organize',...,0]] — both flag 0. index.html:483 defines Ingest as [['Dry-run',...,0],['Ingest now',...,1]] — the writing action flag 1. The a[2] flag drives primary styling in the mods.map render (line 498: accink/acc vs dim/panel2). qa_findings.title: Loop review list has no column headers
qa_findings.impact: Each loop row renders title / artist / role / bpm / score columns, but unlike the Tracks table (which has an explicit header row) the loop list has no header. The bare numeric columns (rounded bpm, 2-decimal score) are unlabeled, so a reviewer must guess what the two right-side numbers mean.

qa_findings.title: Perf stage duration bar is a fixed 100%-width fill — conveys no timing
qa_findings.impact: Each stage row renders a 180px bar with `width:100%` hard-coded regardless of the stage's seconds. The numeric 'Ns' label carries all the information; the bar is decorative and misleadingly implies every stage took the same time. A relative bar (seconds / max-seconds) would make the slow stage obvious, which is the stated purpose of the ledger (app.py:391 already computes top stages by seconds).
qa_findings.fix: Scale bar width to `s.seconds / maxSeconds * 100`% after computing the max across stages.
qa_findings.verify_note: index.html:475 renders each stage bar as `<i style="...width:100%...">` inside a 180px track, hard-coded regardless of s.seconds — the numeric 'Ns' label carries all timing info and the bar misleadingly implies equal durations. app.py:391 already sorts stages by seconds, so a relative (seconds/max) bar is feasible. Cosmetic; confirmed as polish. Note: this is currently moot in practice because Finding 1 keeps the panel empty.

qa_findings.title: A gate-REFUSED plan can still be saved with no guard
qa_findings.where: index.html:404-410 proposePlan (sets window.__plan even when fails.length), 387 savePlan
qa_findings.impact: proposePlan sets window.__plan and renders the rails even when the taste gate REFUSES (meta shows 'REFUSED'), and Save plan has no check for failures. A user can save a plan the engine has declared unrenderable, storing something Render would still ignore anyway.
qa_findings.fix: Either warn on Save when the last proposal was REFUSED, or make clear saved plans are drafts only.
qa_findings.verify_note: propose_plan (app.py:4977-4998) returns {ok:True, taste_gate:...} even when the gate fails (it does not raise; it just reports gate). proposePlan (index.html:404-411) sets window.__plan=j.arrangement whenever j.ok, then labels meta 'REFUSED' if fails.length but keeps the plan. savePlan (387) has no failure check, so a REFUSED plan is fully saveable. Confirmed; polish is appropriate since Render ignores saved plans anyway and this only stores a draft.

Parallelize per-layer librosa transforms (time-stretch/pitch) across cores to lift render-phase CPU from ~1 core.

honest_perf_finding.question: did the model-cache / scaling perf commits speed up renders?
honest_perf_finding.answer: NO measured render speedup. Desktop measured: a cold render is demucs-separation-bound (~6s x N cache-misses). Proof the model wasn't reloaded per separation: a 21-sep cold render took 115s, not the ~300s real per-call reloads would cost.
honest_perf_finding.what_actually_holds: The CONTENT cache (stems reused by pcm_sha) is the real lever: cold ~170s -> warm ~48s ~= 3.5x, and it pre-dates these commits. The commits' real contribution is PLACEMENT (1.7GB cache now on S: NVMe, not C:) + no C: litter + a marginal cross-render model skip in a long-lived server.
honest_perf_finding.to_speed_cold_render: attack the demucs separation itself (smaller/faster model, GPU batching), not caching.
